To record the reclassification of Other Land Improvement to Other Structure:

Discontinue the practice of reverting the full disbursement, including withheld taxes, entirely to Accounts Payable in adherence to Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 47-2023 to ensure proper accounting treatment and compliance with tax withholding regulations; and
Prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the balances of the Due to BIR and Accounts Payable accounts in compliance with IPSAS 1 and its Philippine Application Guidance.
We further recommended the following adjusting journal entries:
To record the reclassification of Accounts Payable to Due to BIR:

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Comply with the procedures and guidelines set in Section 59, Chapter 3, Volume 1 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs to guarantee that financial statements correctly reflect outstanding obligations and ensure the accuracy and reliability of the said accounts in compliance with Item No. 27 of IPSAS 1 and Section 111 of PD No. 1445; and
Prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entries (AJE) to correct the balances of Government Equity and Accounts Payable accounts in compliance with IPSAS 1, PD No. 1445, and the NGAS for LGUs.
We further recommended the following adjusting journal entries:
To record the reclassification of Accounts Payable to Due to BIR:

We recommended that the Management derecognize the twenty-one (21) dead heads of, missing, or can no longer be found breeding stocks, with a total aggregated amount of ₱362,000.00, from Biological Assets and recognize the corresponding amount under Accounts Receivable by preparing the necessary adjusting journal entries to comply with Paragraph 16 of IPSAS 27 and Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1.
We further recommended the following adjusting journal entries:
To record the derecognition of the twenty-one (21) dead, missing, or can no longer be found breeding stocks:

We recommended that the Management direct:
The Municipal Agriculturist to adopt the quoted market prices for cattle and carabaos as the basis for fair value measurement and provide verifiable supporting documentation of, as required by Paragraph 21 of IPSAS 27, except when Paragraph 22 of IPSAS 27 applies; and
The Municipal Accountant should include detailed disclosures in the notes to the financial statements regarding the measurement basis, valuation techniques, key inputs used, and any significant assumptions or estimates made with verifiable supporting documents.

We recommended that the Management:
Direct the Municipal Agriculturist to coordinate with the recipients of the missing or deceased cattle/carabao and secure the necessary supporting documents required under the Memorandum of Agreement, such as veterinary certificates, affidavits, and loss reports, to validate the condition of the breeding stocks and to remain compliant with the stipulations in the Memorandum of Agreement; and
Require the accountable officer to formally justify the status of the unaccounted biological assets and, if applicable, initiate a request for relief from accountability in accordance with Section 73 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that Management require the Accounting Unit:
To implement a standardized property identification system by assigning unique Property Numbers to all PPE items aligned with the General Services Officer’s (GSO) Report on the Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE), following the guidelines set under the One-Time Cleansing of PPE of COA Circular No. 2020-006; and
To take the necessary steps to ensure proper reconciliation of PPE records by updating the PPE Ledger Cards (PPELCs), particularly the Descriptions (should be detailed and specific, incorporating essential information such as brand, size, color, model, and other distinguishing characteristics) and Account Classifications, in accordance with Section 6.3.2(d) of COA Circular No. 2020-006 and Item No. A-09_PPELC of LGU NGAs Volume 2 Forms & Instructions.

We recommended that the Management coordinate with the Provincial Agriculture Office, Isabela Province to initiate the conduct of valuation of the 12 heads of Brahman cattle that was transferred to the Municipal LGU, to ensure accuracy and proper valuation of the account to comply with Paragraph 16 of IPSAS 27 and Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1.

We recommended that the management:
Request clarification from the Provincial Veterinary Office regarding the intent of the transfer of twelve (12) heads Brahman cattle;
Execute a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA), deed of transfer or similar legal document with the Provincial Veterinary Office of Isabela or the appropriate party/unit to formally establish the transfer of ownership of the Brahman cattle, if applicable; and
Require the provision of comprehensive documentation, including a deed of transfer or other formal agreements, that explicitly states the transfer of ownership for any future receipt or transfer of biological assets.

We commended the Management particularly the Municipal Accounting Office personnel, Municipal General Services Office  personnel and the Inventory Team for their commitment and efforts to comply with the conduct of annual physical inventory of all its properties and its reconciliation with the books and records of the Accounting Office, thus ensuring a reliable balance of PPE accounts in the financial statements.

Procure telephone/communication services for cellular/mobile phones in accordance with Appendix 21 of The Updated 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulation of Republic Act 9184.

Official Receipts/ Invoices

Require both the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to prepare Annexes A to E, as assigned to each, together with proper and relevant supporting documents and submit the same to the Auditor in adherence with COA Circular No. 2015-008; and
Enjoin the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officers the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects as required under COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015.

It was also recommended that the management should ensure that all the documentary requirements for infrastructure projects be submitted pursuant to Sections 9.1, 13.1 and 13.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and procurement laws.

We recommended that the management:
Retain ten percent (10%) from every progress payment until fifty percent (50%) of the total value works are completed as required under Sections 6.1 and 6.2, Annex “E” of 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184;
Require the contractors to post warranty security in accordance with Section 62.2.3.3 of the same IRR, which shall remain effective for one (1) year from the date of issuance of Certificate of Final Acceptance; and
Comply with the rules on warranties and all other related requirements provided in 2016 IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Accountable Officers to submit Acknowledgment Receipts for all donations in-kind, prepare submit a Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances and furnish copies to the Municipal Accountant and the Auditor in compliance with COA rules and regulations. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant to ensure that proper recognition and disclosure of donations in-kind are made in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the management ensure that payments for these allowances are supported with relevant and pertinent documents similarly required by COA Circular No. 2012-001 to establish compliance with the conditions set under AO No. 36 dated November 16, 2020 and reiterated under DBM – DOH Joint Circular No. 2, s. 2020 dated November 25, 2020.

We recommended that the management undertake the following:
Minimize the incurrence of excessive and unnecessary meals, snacks and groceries to the extent allowed by law and limit disbursements which are already within the scope of their representation allowances;
Ensure that these expenses are necessary for the conduct of specific projects, programs and activities and shall promote general welfare of its constituents; and
Avoid excessive and unnecessary expenditures for meals, snacks and groceries and those without specific public purpose as these are outright disallowable under COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the Municipal Treasurer and concerned Accountable Officers to produce immediately the undeposited collections of P386,580.96 and the missing accountable forms, submit written explanation together with proofs of actual deposits, if there are any, otherwise Notice of Charge shall be issued.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Require the concerned Accountable Officers to remit all collections under their respective accountabilities; and
Enjoin the accountable officers to submit a written justification for all the missing accountable forms under their custody and unreported/undeclared collections and provide supporting documents, otherwise other sanctions may be recommended for the negligence in the custody and safekeeping of government properties and funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to collect the correct amount of fees, taxes, and charges in accordance with the Ordinance passed by the Sangguniang Bayan and any exemptions/discounts should be specified therewith.

Procurement of goods should be based on relevant characteristics and/or performance requirements and that which achieve the most advantageous price to the government.

observations.managementAction:
The management revisited the programming, budgeting, implementation and the realignment of the 20% Development Fund from 2011-2019 and reconciled from the Audit Observation and here are our findings:
1.	Portion of F. Siquian Street
- In 2012, One hundred thousand pesos (P100,000.00) was appropriated to concrete a portion of F. Siquian St. at Centro 3, Angadanan, Isabela, we only utilized Fifty thousand pesos (P50,000.00) and the project was completed leaving Fifty thousand pesos (P50,000.00) as savings.
2.	Construction of Wet Market
- One million pesos (P1,000,000.00 was appropriated for the construction to finish the wet market but due to financial assistance from senators, only a portion of the appropriated amount was used, leaving Four hundred sixty two thousand one hundred sixty two pesos (P462,162.00) as savings.
3.	Eco-Tourism Site
- We appropriated Two hundred thousand pesos (P200,000.00) in 2013 for Eco-tourism site but we only utilized One hundred eight thousand three hundred pesos (P108,300.00) leaving Ninety-one thousand seven hundred pesos (P91,700.00) as savings.
4.	Rehabilitation of Classroom
Health Facilities Enhancement Program
Women’s Welfare Program
Electrification
Farm to Market Road

These are BUB Projects, there are 14 participating agencies namely, DA, DAR, DENR, DepEd, DILG, DOE, DOLE, DOT, DSWD, DTI, NEA, TESDA, and Philhealth. Each of these agencies offers a menu of projects under which a municipality can avail of based on the LGU’s Local Poverty Reduction Action Plan (LPRAP). These agencies incorporate in their respective annual budget proposals the list of priority programs and projects under BUB for funding by the National Government. We availed of the program but some National agencies did not download their funds to the LGU, making it impossible for us to implement the project.


Farm to Market Road
- This BUB project under the Department of Agriculture (DA). DA completed the project and only one hundred forty thousand three hundred eighty-seven pesos (P140,387.00) was used as counterpart leaving One hundred fifty-nine thousand six hundred (P159,613.00) as savings from the Three hundred thousand pesos (P300,000.00) municipal counterpart appropriated for the purpose.

Construction of Playground Facilities
- The LGU appropriated playground facilities to be constructed at:
1.CalabayanMinanga	P200,000.00
2. La Suerte		P200,000.00

The projects were not implemented because the recipient barangays cannot provide a lot for the construction of the playground facilities as counterpart. The funds are still intact.

Improvement of RHU Water System
- The LGU appropriated Twenty thousand pesos (P20,000.00 for the improvement of RHU water system, several attempts were made to drill for water source but to no avail leaving the project in abeyance.

Construction of Eco-Tourism Site
- The LGU allocated an amount of One million pesos (P1,000,000.00) for the construction of Mural at the river bank of Centro 1 but since nobody knows how to make a Mural in the locality the project was neglected until now, however, the funds are still available

Construction of Comfort Rooms-Wet Market
- In 2014, the LGU appropriated Nine Hundred twenty-three thousand pesos (P923,000.00) in the 20% Development Fund for the construction of paid CR in the Wet Market, but the fund was realigned for the construction of Material Recovery Facility (MRF) which cost the Municipality Seven hundred twenty-one thousand five hundred forty pesos (P721,540.00) leaving a balance of Two hundred one thousand four hundred sixty pesos (P201,460.00)

Bamboo Propagation and Development
- The LGU appropriated One Million pesos (P1,000,000.00) for the Bamboo Propagation and Development but only Five hundred forty-seven thousand nine hundred eighty-two pesos and 65/100 (P547,982.65) was utilized for the purpose leaving Four hundred fifty-two thousand seventeen pesos and 35/100 (P452,017.35) as savings.

Acquisition of Lot for Slaughterhouse
- The LGU appropriated P40,000.00 for the purchase of lot for the slaughterhouse but no available lot in the Poblacion is available for the purpose, so the LGU instead constructed the slaughterhouse at the nursery area.

Construction of Integrated Facilities (P1,500,000.00)
- The appropriation intended for the Integrated Facilities was realigned for the Construction of TB Dots Building.

Based from the explanations above cited, it appears that most of the programmed projects were satisfactorily accomplished and the remaining outstanding were savings from the operations in the implementation of the Projects. Other projects not implemented are due to the failure of the National Agencies like DepEd, DOH, DSWD and DOE to give their counterpart which comprises a large chunk of the project. Construction of Integrated Facilities amounting to P1,500,000.00 was realigned for TB Dots Building also under the Rural Health Unit and the purchase of X-ray facilities is in process. Savings generated from the implementation of different projects shall be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund and continuing appropriation no longer relevant will be evaluated and recommended for reversion.
The audit observation and the recommendations are well-noted and the management will implement the recommendations.

observations.managementAction:
The 2019 GAD Plan and Budget of the municipality underwent a tight screening and review by the DILG before it was incorporated in the budget. It was found compliant in form and content. All the Programs, Projects and Activities were declared responsive to our identified gender issues and/or GAD Mandate. 

Our GAD PPAs as incorporated in our budget are considered as DIRECT APPROPRIATION, which were all 100% GAD responsive as per then the Certificate of Endorsement issued by the DILG for the purpose. The management intentionally used the “Direct Appropriation Method” so as to do away with the tedious and laborious “By Attribution Method” which would inescapably require the HGDG tool as a means of measuring PPAs attributable output score.

As to the RIC, it is a nationwide non-government organization in the Philippines which is rural and barangay based, uplifting the living standard of its members and making them effective and productive partners of the government in community development, hence we have thought that their participation as project beneficiaries has served some of the very purpose of GAD’s fund utilization. The Day Care Workers, on the other hand, are just receiving minimum honoraria from the barangays which they are employed, thus the LGU decided to help them out by giving them additional honoraria for the purpose relative to ECCD law. On the other hand, the Philhealth premium paid by the LGU in 2019 for the health insurance coverage of our indigent constituents was our little way to help the underprivileged and the less fortunate who could not afford to pay for themselves the required premiums. It served as the counterpart of the municipality of which payment was made directly to Philhealth. Lapses in the required additional documentation may be produced, if necessary. This year and for the ensuing year, however, such GAD PPA is no longer included in our GPB as our indigent beneficiaries are now to be taken care by the national government by virtue of RA 11223, otherwise known as the “Universal Health Care Act of 2019”.
The audit observation and recommendations are noted and the management will exert efforts to implement the same.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management exert efforts in reconciling the noted-variances and inconsistencies in the submitted reports; prepare the necessary adjustments and present such pertinent disclosures and other relevant information that will provide more accurate and reliable reports.

We recommended that Management:
Exert efforts to demand continuously and collect the accounts, if the persons accountable may still located or known;
Otherwise, determine existence and collectability of the receivables and assess whether the receivables may be impaired and recognize allowance for impairment in the books, if warranted; and
In case of existence of applicable conditions set forth under Section 7.4 of COA Circular No. 2016-005, submit a written request for write-off to be supported by the required documents specified in Section 8.3 of the same Circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant re-evaluate the unexpended balances of fund transfers that were received from various agencies and if there is a need to use the funds for the projects of the LGU, if provided in the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA), management should make written representations to the Source Agencies for the retention of the funds. Otherwise, management should return the unexpended balances to the concerned Source Agencies and/or remit the same immediately to the National Treasury, as required under the afore-cited rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Management:
Ensure that the unliquidated cash advances shall be settled in the nearest period and/or withhold any amount due from the accountable officers as provided for by Section 9.3.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002, if circumstances may warrant; and
Strictly comply with COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended that the Local School Board, through the Municipal Mayor as Chairman, include in the SEF Budget programs/projects/activities specifically authorized under DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 and implement the same in order to maximize the use of the SEF for its intended purpose.
Likewise, the Local School Board shall have a conscious and concerted effort of observing compliance with the legal and regulatory requirements on SEF budgeting, allocation and utilization and refrain from accommodating expenditures for activities for which the DepEd has the direct mandate to execute.

We recommended that Management:
Appropriate one percent (1%) of its Annual Budget for the Senior Citizen and Person with Disabilities and ensure optimal utilization of the fund to fully uphold the goal of addressing the concerns of senior citizens and persons with disability; and,
Warrant the appointment of OSCA Head and the establishment of PDAO in compliance with the CSC rules, RA 9994 and RA 10070 in order to show basic support and commitment of the LGU to serve and prioritize the interest of the senior citizens and persons with disabilities.

We also recommended that Management identify other projects, activities and programs of disaster preparedness, prevention and mitigation, disaster response and disaster rehabilitation and recovery as listed in NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the heads of the concerned implementing offices to submit (a) list of all on-going government PPAs of the immediately preceding years (2016 and 2017) and those that are to be implemented during the year (2018) and (b) Monthly Monitoring Report in compliance with the above-Circular for review, evaluation and validation of the Audit Team and TAS of the Commission.

As a reiteration of our previous recommendation, the management is enjoined to strictly observe the guidelines in the granting and liquidation of cash advances as provided under COA Circular No. 97-002, refrained from granting additional cash advances to accountable officers whose previous cash advances have not been settled, limit the grants of cash advances to the necessity of occurrence and in accordance with what was allowed by the regulation, and officials and employees should only be allowed to draw cash advance for official travels expenses.

We also recommended that management require the concerned officials and employees to liquidate and settle immediately their outstanding cash advances. Likewise, management is recommended to enforce the immediate liquidation of these cash advances especially those that have been long outstanding thru the issuance of Demand Letters to concerned personnel. Deductions should not be made on a minimal amount and require strict settlement of the balance within a year.

Moreover, balances of cash advances of those officials and employees who are no longer in the service should be reclassified to the account Other Receivables and Demand Letters should be issued.
